- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.

Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,

opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.

Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".

content.text:
`m5_workend(workid, threadid)` marks the end of an annotated "region of interest" (ROI). In `src/sim/pseudo_inst.cc:560` `workend()`:

1. If `system.exit_on_work_items` is set, calls `exitSimLoop("workend", workid)` - returns control to Python with exit cause `"workend"` so the script can dump stats / switch CPUs / take checkpoint.
2. Otherwise:
   - `cpu->workItemEnd()` + `sys->workItemEnd(threadid, workid)` - bookkeeping for per-CPU / per-system work-item counters (used by `WorkItems` stats).
   - If `work_item_id == -1` or matches `workid`, increments `systemWorkEndCount` and may:
     - exit with `"checkpoint"` when `work_cpus_ckpt_count` CPUs have hit the mark (`work_cpus_ckpt_count`),
     - exit with `"checkpoint"` when total ends == `work_end_ckpt_count`,
     - exit with `"work items exit count reached"` when total ends == `work_end_exit_count`.

Paired with `m5_workbegin`, it delimits the ROI so gem5 can switch CPU models, reset/dump stats, or checkpoint exactly at region boundaries instead of arbitrary tick counts.

The help string in `workend.cc:50` ("Exit immediately") is a copy-paste bug from `exit.cc`; the actual behavior is the work-item logic above.

content.text:
Yes. `m5_workend` is just an exit hook - what happens on that exit is whatever the Python config decides. Switching from O3 back to AtomicSimpleCPU is a valid choice.

How it works:
1. Guest executes `m5_workend` -> `pseudo_inst.cc:567` calls `exitSimLoop("workend", ...)`.
2. Control returns to Python. `exit_event.py:79-80` maps the string `"workend"` to `ExitEvent.WORKEND`.
3. Your handler runs. In `x86-gapbs-benchmarks.py:147-153`, the `WORKBEGIN` handler calls `simulator.switch_processor()` to go KVM -> Timing. The matching `WORKEND` handler at line 160-168 just dumps stats and exits, but it could equally call `switch_processor()` again to go back to AtomicSimple/KVM for the post-ROI region.

A common pattern in detailed-warmup studies:
- Boot: KVM (fast).
- `workbegin` -> switch to O3 (detailed, slow).
- `workend` -> switch back to KVM/Atomic to fast-forward past teardown, OR just `m5.stats.dump()` and exit.

The switching is not done by `m5_workend` itself; it requires a `SimpleSwitchableProcessor` (or manual `m5.switchCpus`) and the Python handler to call `switch_processor()`. `m5_workend` only provides the synchronization point.

file.content:
def dump(roots=None, message=""):
    """Dump all statistics data to the registered outputs

    Args:
        roots: Optional list of roots to dump
        message: Optional message to include in text output headers
    """

    all_roots = []
    if roots is not None:
        all_roots.extend(roots)
    global global_dump_roots
    all_roots.extend(global_dump_roots)

    now = m5.curTick()
    global lastDump
    assert lastDump <= now
    new_dump = lastDump != now
    lastDump = now

    # Don't allow multiple global stat dumps in the same tick. It's
    # still possible to dump a multiple sub-trees.
    if not new_dump and not all_roots:
        return

    # Only prepare stats the first time we dump them in the same tick.
    if new_dump:
        _m5_stats.processDumpQueue()
        # Notify new-style stats group that we are about to dump stats.
        sim_root = Root.getInstance()
        if sim_root:
            sim_root.preDumpStats()
        prepare()

    for output in outputList:
        if isinstance(output, JsonOutputVistor):
            if not all_roots:
                output.dump(Root.getInstance())
            else:
                output.dump(all_roots)
        else:
            if output.valid():
                output.begin(message)
                _dump_to_visitor(output, roots=all_roots)
                output.end()


def reset():
    """Reset all statistics to the base state"""

content.text:
`m5.stats.dump()` writes the current values of every registered statistic to the configured output files (default `m5out/stats.txt`, optionally JSON via the H5/Json visitors). It does not print to stdout and does not reset the counters - use `m5.stats.reset()` for that.

What's in `stats.txt` (one section per `dump()` call, delimited by `---------- Begin Simulation Statistics ----------` / `---------- End ...`):

- Global sim state: `simSeconds`, `simTicks`, `simInsts`, `simOps`, `hostSeconds`, `hostTickRate`, `hostMemory`.
- Per-SimObject stats, fully hierarchical by path. Examples:
  - `board.processor.cores0.core.numCycles`, `...exec_context.thread_0.numInsts`, `committedInsts`, `cpi`, `ipc`.
  - O3 internals: `iew.exec_stores`, `rob.reads`, `lsq0.forwLoads`, `commit.branchMispredicts`, `fetch.icacheStallCycles`.
  - Caches: `l1d.overallHits::total`, `overallMisses`, `overallMissRate`, `overallAvgMissLatency`, `replacements`, `tags.tagAccesses`.
  - Memory ctrl: `mem_ctrl.bytesRead`, `bw_total`, `rdQLenPdf::*`, `bytesPerActivate::*`.
  - TLBs, branch predictor, prefetcher, coherent xbar, etc.
- Distributions/histograms print as `mean / stdev / min / max / samples / bucket counts`.
- Vector stats print `::idx` per element plus `::total`.

So in the gapbs example, `m5.stats.dump()` at `workend` captures everything accumulated since the previous `reset()` at `workbegin` - i.e., ROI-only numbers. The file is append-mode, so each ROI gets its own delimited block.

content.text:
Two separate mechanisms tied to the same hook:

## 1. C++ auto-checkpoint via System params

When `m5_workbegin`/`m5_workend` fires and `exit_on_work_items = False`, `pseudo_inst.cc` increments per-system counters and may emit `exitSimLoop("checkpoint")`. The string `"checkpoint"` is special - the Python run loop treats it as "save a checkpoint and resume", not "stop".

Counters (in `System.py:131-144`):

| Param | Fires from | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `work_begin_ckpt_count` | `workbegin` | Checkpoint when N-th begin reached |
| `work_end_ckpt_count` | `workend` | Checkpoint when N-th end reached |
| `work_cpus_ckpt_count` | both | Checkpoint when N distinct CPUs have hit a marker |
| `work_begin_exit_count` | `workbegin` | Exit (not checkpoint) at N-th begin |
| `work_end_exit_count` | `workend` | Exit at N-th end |
| `work_item_id` | filter | If set, only that workid counts; `-1` = any |

So: `--param 'system.work_begin_ckpt_count=1'` gives you a checkpoint exactly at ROI entry. Restoring from that checkpoint lets you start detailed simulation right at the ROI without re-running boot/setup every time. This is the classic SimPoint-style flow.

## 2. Python-controlled via exit_on_work_items

If `exit_on_work_items = True`, the C++ side skips all the counter logic (`pseudo_inst.cc:503-506`) and emits `exitSimLoop("workbegin"|"workend", workid)`. Control returns to your Python script - now *you* decide whether to call `m5.checkpoint(dir)`, switch CPUs, dump stats, etc. The standard-library `WorkBeginExitHandler`/`WorkEndExitHandler` use this path.

## Typical pipeline

1. Run with KVM/Atomic until `m5_workbegin` -> Python handler calls `m5.checkpoint("roi_entry.cpt")`, then `exit`.
2. New run: `restore_from_path="roi_entry.cpt"`, swap in O3 -> simulate until `m5_workend`.

The checkpoint captures full architectural state (regs, memory, devices) but *not* microarchitectural state (caches, ROB, branch predictor) - that's why people typically warm up after restore before measuring.
